lines.translation: Bai Ying'ai, what day of the week is September 12th?
Really? How old are you this year?
I'll treat you to a meal on Thursday, how about that?
That's great! Thank you, thank you.
Do you like to eat Chinese food or American food?
I am British, but I like to eat Chinese food.
Okay, see you on Thursday evening.

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: Chinese time expressions proceed from the largest to the smallest unit, e.g., 二〇一九年八月十二日晚上七点 (èr líng yī jiǔ nián bāyuè shí’èr rì wǎnshang qī diǎn) (2019, August 12, 7:00 p.m.).

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: These nouns do not require a measure word because they function as measure words on their own.

language_notes.label: c
language_notes.explanation_en: Say this to find out someone's age. If you're asking a child, use 你今年几岁? (Nǐ jīnnián jǐ suì?). To ask an older person, use the polite forms 您多大年纪了? (Nín duō dà niánjì le?) or 您多大岁数了? (Nín duō dà suìshù le?).

language_notes.label: d
language_notes.explanation_en: State your age by saying 我十八岁 (wǒ shíbā suì) (I'm eighteen years old). The verb 是 (shì) is usually not needed, and the word 岁 (suì) (years of age) can often be dropped. However, if the age is ten or under, the word 岁 (suì) cannot be omitted: ※ 我十 or ※ 我八. Note that it is incorrect to say, ※ 我十八年.

language_notes.label: e
language_notes.explanation_en: When 太…了 (tài ... le) is used in an exclamation (as in the case here), the stress usually falls on 太 (tài), and it can typically be translated as “so” or “really.”

language_notes.label: f
language_notes.explanation_en: To express gratitude, say 谢谢 (xièxie), or 谢谢, 谢谢 (xièxie, xièxie), which is more polite and exuberant.

grammar_sections.title: Numbers up to 100
grammar_sections.explanation_en: The characters below serve as the basis for the Chinese numeration system. All other double-digit numbers can be formed using numbers from 一 (yī) to 十 (shí), following the patterns below. When counting by hundreds, the pattern is single digit + 百 (bǎi) (hundred), e.g.: 一百 (yībǎi) (one hundred), 二百 / 两百 (èrbǎi/liǎngbǎi) (two hundred).

patterns.meaning_en: For numbers 21-99 (excluding multiples of ten). Application: 21, 22–98, 99.

grammar_sections.title: Dates
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Days of the week: 星期几 (xīngqījǐ) is the standard way to ask the day of the week. To answer the question, simply replace the word 几 (jǐ) (how many) with the number indicating the day of the week. There are varying degrees of formality: 周 (zhōu) (week) is formal, 星期 (xīngqī) (week) is standard, and 礼拜 (lǐbài) (week) is informal. Dates: In spoken Chinese, 号 (hào) (number) is used to refer to dates. However, in written Chinese, 日 (rì) (day) is used instead. Year: The word 年 (nián) (year) always follows the number referring to a specific year. Years are read one digit at a time. Word order for dates: To specify a date in Chinese, observe the following order: Year, Month, Day, Day of the Week.

patterns.pattern: X 年 X 月 X 号/日 星期 X
patterns.pattern_pinyin: X nián X yuè X hào/rì xīngqī X
patterns.meaning_en: Year, Month, Day, Day of the Week

usage_notes: The expression 礼拜 (lǐbài) is generally used in spoken Chinese and is more colloquial than 星期 (xīngqī).
星期日 (xīngqīrì) is used more in written Chinese, whereas 星期天 (xīngqītiān) is used more in spoken Chinese.
周末 (zhōumò) is used in standard, informal, and formal Chinese.
In spoken Chinese, 号 (hào) is used to refer to dates. In written Chinese, 日 (rì) is used instead.
The word 年 (nián) always follows the number referring to a specific year. Years are read one digit at a time.

grammar_sections.title: Time
grammar_sections.explanation_en: The terms used to tell the time are: 点 (diǎn)/点钟 (diǎnzhōng) (o'clock), 半 (bàn) (half hour), 刻 (kè) (quarter hour), and 分 (fēn) (minute).
usage_notes: 钟 (zhōng) can be omitted from 点钟 (diǎnzhōng).
When telling the time, 〇 (líng) (zero) is usually added before a single-digit number for minutes, e.g., 两点〇五分 (liǎng diǎn líng wǔ fēn) (2:05).
分 (fēn) can be omitted from the end of the expression if the number for the minutes appears in two syllables. 分 (fēn) has to be added if the number for the minutes appears in one syllable.
Observe the temporal progression from general to specific and from largest to smallest unit.

common_errors.explanation_en: 分 (fēn) has to be added if the number for the minutes appears in one syllable.
分 (fēn) has to be added if the number for the minutes appears in one syllable.
两刻 (two quarters) is not used.

grammar_sections.title: Pronouns as modifiers and the particle 的 (de) (II)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: When personal pronouns such as 我 (wǒ) (I), 我们 (wǒmen) (we), 你 (nǐ) (you), 他 (tā) (he), and 她 (tā) (she) are followed by a term indicating a close personal relationship, the particle 的 (de) can be omitted, e.g., 我妈妈 (wǒ māma) (my mother), 你弟弟 (nǐ dìdi) (your younger brother), 我们家 (wǒmen jiā) (our family). Otherwise 的 (de) is generally required, e.g., 他的医生 (tā de yīshēng) (his doctor), 我的照片 (wǒ de zhàopiàn) (my photo).

grammar_sections.title: The sentence structure of 我请你吃饭
grammar_sections.explanation_en: In the sentence 我请你吃饭 (wǒ qǐng nǐ chī fàn) (I will treat you to dinner), 你 (nǐ) (you) is the object of the verb 请 (qǐng) (to treat) as well as the subject of the second verb, 吃 (chī) (to eat).

examples.hanzi: 明天李先生请你吃中国菜。
examples.pinyin: Míngtiān Lǐ xiānsheng qǐng nǐ chī Zhōngguó cài.
examples.translation: Mr. Li is inviting you to have Chinese food tomorrow.

examples.hanzi: 今天晚上我请你和你妹妹吃美国菜, 怎么样?
examples.pinyin: Jīntiān wǎnshang wǒ qǐng nǐ hé nǐ mèimei chī Měiguó cài, zěnmeyàng?
examples.translation: I'll treat you and your younger sister to American food tonight. How about it?

grammar_sections.title: Alternative questions
grammar_sections.explanation_en: The structure (是)……还是…… ([shì]... háishi...) (...or...) is used to form an alternative question. If there is another verb used in the predicate, the first 是 (shì) (to be) can often be omitted.

examples.hanzi: 你哥哥是老师还是学生？
examples.pinyin: Nǐ gēge shì lǎoshī háishi xuésheng?
examples.translation: Is your older brother a teacher or a student?

examples.hanzi: 他（是）喜欢吃中国菜还是喜欢吃美国菜？
examples.pinyin: Tā (shì) xǐhuan chī Zhōngguó cài háishi xǐhuan chī Měiguó cài?
examples.translation: Does he like to eat Chinese or American food?

examples.hanzi: 中国菜、美国菜他都喜欢（吃）。
examples.pinyin: Zhōngguó cài, Měiguó cài tā dōu xǐhuan (chī).
examples.translation: He likes both Chinese food and American food.

examples.hanzi: 你是中国人还是美国人？
examples.pinyin: Nǐ shì Zhōngguó rén háishi Měiguó rén?
Tā (shì) xìng Gāo háishi xìng Wáng?
examples.translation: Is his family name Gao or Wang?

examples.hanzi: 他不姓高，也不姓王。他姓李。
examples.pinyin: Tā bú xìng Gāo, yě bú xìng Wáng. Tā xìng Lǐ.
examples.translation: His family name is not Gao or Wang. His family name is Li.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to ask for the date and the day of the week.

narration.subtitle_en: The dialogue begins with Gao Wenzhong asking a question. He says: [zh:白英爱,九月十二号是星期几]. He's asking, 'Bai Ying'ai, what day of the week is September 12th?'. Let's break this down. [zh:九月] is September, literally 'ninth month'. [zh:十二号] is the 12th day. And the key question phrase is [zh:星期几], which means 'what day of the week?'. Notice the time expression goes from largest to smallest: month, then day.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 白英爱,九月十二号是星期几?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: bái yīng ài, jiǔ yuè shí èr hào shì xīng qī jǐ?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Bai Ying'ai, what day of the week is September 12th?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'ninth month'. Chinese months are formed by a number followed by 月 (yuè).
A measure word used for dates in spoken Chinese.
The standard way to ask for the day of the week. 几 (jǐ) is a question word for numbers.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a calendar page flipping to September 12th, with '星期几?' highlighted.

segments.segment_title: Answering the Day of the Week
segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to state the day of the week.

narration.subtitle_en: Bai Ying'ai gives a direct answer: [zh:是星期四]. This means 'It's Thursday.' The structure is very simple. You just replace the question word [zh:几] from the question with the number for the day. Since Thursday is the fourth day of the week in this system, she says [zh:星期四]. The word [zh:是] means 'is' or 'to be', confirming the fact.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: shì xīng qī sì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: It's Thursday.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'week four'. Days of the week are formed by 星期 (xīngqī) followed by a number from one to six, and 日 (rì) or 天 (tiān) for Sunday.

segments.visual_notes: Show a weekly calendar with Thursday highlighted.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to say that a certain day is your birthday.

narration.subtitle_en: Gao Wenzhong then reveals why he's asking. He says, [zh:那天是我的生日]. This translates to 'That day is my birthday.' Let's look at the parts. [zh:那] means 'that', and [zh:天] means 'day', so [zh:那天] is 'that day'. [zh:是] is 'is'. [zh:我的] is the possessive 'my'. And the new word [zh:生日] means 'birthday'.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nà tiān shì wǒ de shēng rì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: That day is my birthday.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to a day. It doesn't require a measure word.
Composed of 生 (shēng), meaning 'to be born', and 日 (rì), meaning 'day'.

segments.visual_notes: An icon of a birthday cake or gift appears next to the sentence.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to express surprise and ask for someone's age.

narration.subtitle_en: Bai Ying'ai is surprised and asks, [zh:是吗?你今年多大]. The first part, [zh:是吗], is a common way to say 'Really?' or 'Is that so?'. The second part, [zh:你今年多大], is how you ask someone's age. [zh:你] is 'you', [zh:今年] means 'this year', and the question phrase [zh:多大] means 'how old'. This is a standard and polite way to ask an adult peer their age.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: shì ma? nǐ jīnnián duō dà?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Really? How old are you this year?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Composed of 今 (jīn), meaning 'present' or 'this', and 年 (nián), meaning 'year'.
A question phrase used to ask about age. 多 (duō) means 'how' and 大 (dà) means 'big' or 'old'.

segments.visual_notes: A question mark icon animates next to '多大'.

segments.segment_title: Different Ways to Ask for Age
segments.teaching_goal: Understand the appropriate phrases to use when asking for someone's age based on who you are talking to.

narration.subtitle_en: In Chinese, the way you ask for someone's age depends on who you're talking to. For a peer or someone your age, [zh:你今年多大] is perfect. If you're asking a child, usually under 10, you should use [zh:你今年几岁]. For an older person, it's more respectful to use a polite form like [zh:您多大年纪了]. Using the right phrase shows respect and cultural awareness.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你今年多大? / 你今年几岁? / 您多大年纪了?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nǐ jīnnián duō dà? / nǐ jīnnián jǐ suì? / nín duō dà niánjì le?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: How old are you? (For peers / children / elders)
on_screen_text.notes: Choose the right phrase based on the person's age.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The general way to ask for age.
Used when asking the age of young children, typically under 10.
A respectful term for age, used when asking elders.

segments.visual_notes: Show three icons: one for a peer, one for a child, and one for an elderly person, each with the appropriate Chinese phrase next to it.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to state your age.

narration.subtitle_en: Gao Wenzhong's answer is simple and direct: [zh:十八岁]. This means 'Eighteen years old.' [zh:十八] is the number eighteen, and [zh:岁] is the measure word for years of age. When stating your age, you typically say the number followed by [zh:岁]. Note that the verb [zh:是] is usually not needed here. You just say the number and [zh:岁].

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: shíbā suì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Eighteen years old.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The number 18, formed by 十 (shí, ten) and 八 (bā, eight).
The measure word for age.

segments.visual_notes: The number '18' appears on screen and transforms into the characters '十八'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the sentence structure for inviting someone to do something.

narration.subtitle_en: Now, Gao Wenzhong makes an invitation. He says, [zh:我星期四请你吃饭,怎么样]. This means 'I'll treat you to a meal on Thursday, how about that?'. The key structure here is [zh:请你吃饭], 'to treat you to a meal'. The verb [zh:请] means 'to invite' or 'to treat'. He ends with [zh:怎么样], a very useful phrase that means 'how about it?' or 'what do you think?', used to ask for someone's opinion on a suggestion.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我星期四请你吃饭,怎么样?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ xīngqīsì qǐng nǐ chīfàn, zěnmeyàng?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I'll treat you to a meal on Thursday, how about that?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb used when one person is paying for another or formally inviting them.
A common verb phrase composed of 吃 (chī), 'to eat', and 饭 (fàn), 'rice' or 'meal'.
A phrase used at the end of a statement to ask for an opinion or agreement.

segments.visual_notes: An icon of two people at a dinner table, with one person gesturing to pay.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to enthusiastically accept an invitation and express thanks.

narration.subtitle_en: Bai Ying'ai is very happy to accept. She says, [zh:太好了! 谢谢, 谢谢]. The phrase [zh:太好了] means 'That's great!' or 'Excellent!'. The [zh:太...了] structure is often used to express a high degree of an adjective. Then she says [zh:谢谢] twice. Repeating 'thank you' like this, [zh:谢谢, 谢谢], makes it sound more enthusiastic and sincere than just saying it once.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 太好了! 谢谢, 谢谢。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: tài hǎo le! xièxie, xièxie.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: That's great! Thank you, thank you.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An exclamation using the '太...了' (tài...le) pattern to mean 'so good' or 'excellent'.
The standard way to express thanks. Repeating it adds emphasis and sincerity.

segments.visual_notes: An animated thumbs-up or smiley face icon appears next to '太好了!'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to ask an 'A or B' alternative question using '还是'.

narration.subtitle_en: Gao Wenzhong then asks about her preference: [zh:你喜欢吃中国菜还是美国菜]. This is an alternative question, meaning 'Do you like to eat Chinese food or American food?'. The key word here is [zh:还是], which means 'or' and is used specifically in questions that offer a choice. The structure is 'Subject + Verb + Option A + [zh:还是] + Option B'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你喜欢吃中国菜还是美国菜?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nǐ xǐhuan chī zhōngguó cài háishi měiguó cài?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Do you like to eat Chinese food or American food?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb used to express preference or fondness for something.
Can refer to a specific dish or a country's cuisine, like 中国菜 (Chinese food).
Used to connect two alternative options in a question.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of Chinese food on one side and American food (like a burger) on the other, with '还是' in the middle.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to use '可是' to connect two contrasting ideas.

narration.subtitle_en: Bai Ying'ai replies, [zh:我是英国人, 可是我喜欢吃中国菜]. This means, 'I am British, but I like to eat Chinese food.' She first states her nationality, [zh:我是英国人]. Then she uses the word [zh:可是], which means 'but' or 'however', to introduce a contrasting preference. This is a useful way to connect two different pieces of information about yourself.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我是英国人, 可是我喜欢吃中国菜。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ shì yīngguó rén, kěshì wǒ xǐhuan chī zhōngguó cài.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I am British, but I like to eat Chinese food.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Composed of 英国 (yīngguó), 'Britain', and 人 (rén), 'person'.
A conjunction used to introduce a clause that contrasts with the preceding one.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to make a simple confirmation.

narration.subtitle_en: Gao Wenzhong confirms the plan with a simple sentence: [zh:好,我们吃中国菜]. This means, 'Okay, let's eat Chinese food.' The word [zh:好] here functions as 'okay' or 'good', showing agreement. [zh:我们] means 'we', so [zh:我们吃中国菜] sets the plan for both of them. It's a clear and concise way to finalize a decision.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hǎo, wǒmen chī zhōngguó cài.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Okay, let's eat Chinese food.

segments.visual_notes: A checkmark icon appears to signify agreement and confirmation.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to ask 'what time' using '几点'.

narration.subtitle_en: Now that the day and type of food are decided, Bai Ying'ai asks about the time: [zh:星期四几点]. This is a shortened question for 'What time on Thursday?'. The key phrase is [zh:几点]. [zh:几] asks 'how many' and [zh:点] is the measure word for 'o'clock'. So, [zh:几点] literally means 'how many o'clocks?' and is the standard way to ask 'what time?'.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xīngqīsì jǐ diǎn?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: What time on Thursday?

highlight_words.explanation_en: The question phrase for asking the time. Composed of 几 (jǐ), 'how many', and 点 (diǎn), 'o'clock'.

segments.visual_notes: A clock icon with a question mark inside it.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to suggest a time, including 'half past'.

narration.subtitle_en: Gao Wenzhong suggests a time: [zh:七点半怎么样]. This means 'How about half past seven?'. [zh:七点] is 'seven o'clock', and [zh:半] means 'half'. So, [zh:七点半] is 'seven and a half', or 7:30. He again uses the phrase [zh:怎么样] to make a suggestion and ask for her agreement. This is a very common and useful pattern for making plans.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: qī diǎn bàn zěnmeyàng?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: How about half past seven?

highlight_words.explanation_en: The number seven, 七 (qī), followed by 点 (diǎn), 'o'clock'.
Used after the hour to indicate 'half past', as in 7:30.

segments.visual_notes: A clock face animates to show 7:30.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to confirm a plan and use the phrase 'see you'.

narration.subtitle_en: Bai Ying'ai agrees and confirms the meeting time. She says, [zh:好,星期四晚上见]. This means 'Okay, see you on Thursday evening.' [zh:好] is 'okay'. [zh:星期四晚上] specifies 'Thursday evening'. The final word, [zh:见], is very important. It means 'to see' or 'to meet'. So the whole phrase is a common way to say 'see you then'.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hǎo, xīngqīsì wǎnshang jiàn.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Okay, see you on Thursday evening.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A time word that specifies the part of the day.
Used at the end of a phrase to mean 'see you'. For example, 明天见 (míngtiān jiàn) means 'see you tomorrow'.

segments.visual_notes: A calendar icon for Thursday and a moon icon for evening are shown.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the standard way to say goodbye.

narration.subtitle_en: Finally, Gao Wenzhong says goodbye with the classic phrase: [zh:再见]. This is the most common way to say 'goodbye' in Chinese. If we look at the characters, [zh:再] means 'again' and [zh:见] means 'to see'. So, literally, it means 'see you again'. It's a simple and polite way to end a conversation.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zàijiàn!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Goodbye!

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally means 'see you again', from 再 (zài), 'again', and 见 (jiàn), 'to see'.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students how to recognize, pronounce, and write the basic Chinese numbers from 0 to 10.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's start with a fundamental building block of Chinese: numbers. Mastering the numbers from zero to ten is essential, as all other numbers are built upon them. We'll look at each character, its pinyin pronunciation, and its English meaning. Pay close attention to the tones, as they are crucial for being understood correctly. For example, the number four, [zh:四], and the number ten, [zh:十], have similar sounds but different tones.

segments.visual_notes: Display a grid of characters. As the narrator speaks, highlight each number card in sequence.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the patterns for forming two-digit numbers in Chinese, up to 100.

narration.subtitle_en: Now that you know the basic numbers, let's see how to form larger numbers. The system is very logical. For numbers 11 to 19, you use the pattern 'ten plus digit'. For example, fifteen is 'ten-five', [zh:十五]. For multiples of ten, like twenty or thirty, the pattern is 'digit plus ten'. So, forty is 'four-ten', [zh:四十]. For other numbers, you combine these patterns. For instance, eighty-three is 'eight-ten-three', [zh:八十三]. In our dialogue, Gao Wenzhong says he is [zh:十八岁], eighteen years old, which follows the 'ten-eight' pattern.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the table rows to appear one by one as the narrator explains each pattern. Highlight the example from the dialogue.

content.title: Forming Numbers 11–99
content.note: For two hundred, both 二百 (èrbǎi) and 两百 (liǎngbǎi) are used.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Dates - Months & Days of the Week
segments.teaching_goal: To teach the vocabulary for months and days of the week.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's learn how to talk about dates. In Chinese, this is also very systematic. To say the months, you simply combine a number from one to twelve with the word for month, [zh:月]. So, January is 'one-month', [zh:一月], and September is 'nine-month', [zh:九月], as we heard in the dialogue. For days of the week, you use the word [zh:星期] followed by a number from one to six. For example, Monday is [zh:星期一]. The only exception is Sunday, which can be [zh:星期天] or [zh:星期日].

segments.visual_notes: Show two separate grids. First for months, then for days of the week. Highlight 九月 and 星期四 as they are from the dialogue.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the correct word order for stating dates in Chinese and how to ask for the day of the week.

narration.subtitle_en: A key rule in Chinese is that time expressions go from the largest unit to the smallest. This is the opposite of English. So, the order is always Year, Month, Day, and then Day of the Week. For example, Friday, July 26, 2019 would be said as '2019 year, 7 month, 26 day, Friday'. To ask 'what day of the week is it?', you use the phrase [zh:星期几]. In the dialogue, Gao Wenzhong asks [zh:九月十二号是星期几], which follows this pattern perfectly: 'September 12th is what day of the week?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: 九月十二号是星期几？ A: 是星期四。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: jiǔ yuè shí èr hào shì xīng qī jǐ? shì xīng qī sì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: What day of the week is September 12th? A: It's Thursday.
on_screen_text.notes: Word Order: Year → Month → Day → Day of the Week

grammar_points.pattern: [Year] 年 [Month] 月 [Day] 号/日 星期 [Number]
grammar_points.explanation_en: The standard word order for dates in Chinese, moving from the largest unit (year) to the smallest (day of the week).
The question form to ask for the day of the week. 几 (jǐ) is the question word for numbers.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the word order rule on screen. Then show the dialogue question and highlight the components matching the rule.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students how to ask for and state the time in Chinese, using key terms like 点, 半, and 刻.

narration.subtitle_en: Telling time in Chinese uses a few key words. The word for 'o'clock' is [zh:点]. For 'half past', you use [zh:半]. So, in the dialogue, when Gao Wenzhong suggests [zh:七点半], he means 'seven-thirty'. To ask 'what time?', you use [zh:几点]. Bai Ying'ai asks [zh:星期四几点], 'Thursday, what time?'. You can also specify parts of the day, like [zh:晚上] for evening, before the time. So, [zh:星期四晚上见] means 'See you Thursday evening'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: 星期四几点？ A: 七点半怎么样？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xīngqīsì jǐ diǎn? qī diǎn bàn zěnmeyàng?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: What time on Thursday? A: How about half past seven?
on_screen_text.notes: Key time words: 点 (diǎn) o'clock, 半 (bàn) half, 刻 (kè) quarter, 分 (fēn) minute.

grammar_points.explanation_en: The question 'What time?'
States the hour, e.g., 七点 (qī diǎn) - 7 o'clock.
States 'half past the hour', e.g., 七点半 (qī diǎn bàn) - 7:30.

segments.visual_notes: Show a clock face and animate the hands to show 7:30 as the narrator explains. Highlight the key terms 点 and 半.

segments.teaching_goal: To explain when the possessive particle 的 (de) can be omitted.

narration.subtitle_en: The particle [zh:的] is used to show possession, similar to 's in English. We see it in the dialogue when Gao Wenzhong says [zh:我的生日], 'my birthday'. However, there's an important exception. When you are talking about a close personal relationship, like family members, you can often omit the [zh:的]. For example, you can say [zh:我妈妈] for 'my mom' instead of [zh:我的妈妈]. But for things like birthdays or professions, the [zh:的] is usually required.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ de shēngrì
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: my birthday
on_screen_text.notes: Omit 的 (de) for close relationships: 我妈妈 (wǒ māma), but keep it for others: 我的医生 (wǒ de yīshēng).

grammar_points.explanation_en: Standard possessive structure. Example: 我的生日 (wǒ de shēngrì).
The particle 的 (de) is often omitted. Example: 我妈妈 (wǒ māma).

segments.visual_notes: Show the phrase '我的生日'. Then, show a split screen comparing '我妈妈' (correct) and '我的医生' (correct) to illustrate the rule.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the sentence structure for inviting or treating someone to do something.

narration.subtitle_en: The verb [zh:请] is very useful. It can mean 'please', but here it means 'to invite' or 'to treat'. The sentence structure is 'Subject + [zh:请] + Object + Verb Phrase'. In the dialogue, Gao Wenzhong says [zh:我星期四请你吃饭]. Here, 'I' is the subject, '[zh:请]' is the main verb, 'you' is the object of [zh:请], and 'eat a meal' is the action he is treating you to. Notice that 'you' is both the one being invited and the one who will perform the action of eating.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ xīngqīsì qǐng nǐ chīfàn.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I'll treat you to a meal on Thursday.
on_screen_text.notes: Structure: Subject + 请 (qǐng) + Object + Verb Phrase

grammar_points.explanation_en: This structure is used when someone treats or invites someone else to do something. The object (O) is both the recipient of the invitation and the subject of the verb phrase (VP).

segments.visual_notes: Break down the sentence '我星期四请你吃饭' into its grammatical parts (Subject, Time, Verb, Object, Action) and color-code them.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Alternative Questions with 还是 (háishi)
segments.teaching_goal: To teach how to form 'A or B' questions using 还是 (háishi).

narration.subtitle_en: To ask a question that offers a choice, you use the word [zh:还是], which means 'or'. The structure is 'Option A + [zh:还是] + Option B?'. In the dialogue, Gao Wenzhong asks [zh:你喜欢吃中国菜还是美国菜], 'Do you like to eat Chinese food or American food?'. This is different from a yes/no question. You are expecting the listener to choose one of the options you provided. It's a very common and useful way to ask questions.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你喜欢吃中国菜还是美国菜？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nǐ xǐhuan chī zhōngguó cài háishi měiguó cài?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Do you like to eat Chinese food or American food?

grammar_points.explanation_en: Used to form an alternative question, where the listener is expected to choose between the given options.

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 你是老师还是学生？ A: 我是学生。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Nǐ shì lǎoshī háishi xuésheng? Wǒ shì xuésheng.
grammar_points.explanation_en: An example of an alternative question and a possible answer.

segments.visual_notes: Visually separate the two choices '中国菜' and '美国菜' on screen with the word '还是' in the middle, creating a visual choice.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key vocabulary, phrases, and grammar points from the lesson.

narration.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned in this lesson. We learned how to ask for the date and time, how to state our age, how to invite someone out for a meal, and how to ask questions offering a choice. Take a moment to look at these key phrases. Practice them, and you'll be ready to make plans with your friends in Chinese!

Let's break this down. [zh:九月] is September, literally 'ninth month'. [zh:十二号] is the 12th day. And the key question phrase is [zh:星期几], which means 'what day of the week?'. Notice the time expression goes from largest to smallest: month, then day.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to state the day of the week.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Bai Ying'ai gives a direct answer: [zh:是星期四]. This means 'It's Thursday.' The structure is very simple. You just replace the question word [zh:几] from the question with the number for the day. Since Thursday is the fourth day of the week in this system, she says [zh:星期四]. The word [zh:是] means 'is' or 'to be', confirming the fact.

[zh:那] means 'that', and [zh:天] means 'day', so [zh:那天] is 'that day'. [zh:是] is 'is'. [zh:我的] is the possessive 'my'. And the new word [zh:生日] means 'birthday'.

This is a standard and polite way to ask an adult peer their age.

segments.segment_title: Different Ways to Ask for Age
segments.teaching_goal: Understand the appropriate phrases to use when asking for someone's age based on who you are talking to.

narration_track.subtitle_en: In Chinese, the way you ask for someone's age depends on who you're talking to. For a peer or someone your age, [zh:你今年多大] is perfect. If you're asking a child, usually under 10, you should use [zh:你今年几岁]. For an older person, it's more respectful to use a polite form like [zh:您多大年纪了]. Using the right phrase shows respect and cultural awareness.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to use '可是' to connect two contrasting ideas.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Bai Ying'ai replies, [zh:我是英国人, 可是我喜欢吃中国菜]. This means, 'I am British, but I like to eat Chinese food.' She first states her nationality, [zh:我是英国人]. Then she uses the word [zh:可是], which means 'but' or 'however', to introduce a contrasting preference. This is a useful way to connect two different pieces of information about yourself.

content.focus_text: 我是英国人, 可是我喜欢吃中国菜。

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to make a simple confirmation.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Gao Wenzhong confirms the plan with a simple sentence: [zh:好,我们吃中国菜]. This means, 'Okay, let's eat Chinese food.' The word [zh:好] here functions as 'okay' or 'good', showing agreement. [zh:我们] means 'we', so [zh:我们吃中国菜] sets the plan for both of them. It's a clear and concise way to finalize a decision.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to ask 'what time' using '几点'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now that the day and type of food are decided, Bai Ying'ai asks about the time: [zh:星期四几点]. This is a shortened question for 'What time on Thursday?'. The key phrase is [zh:几点]. [zh:几] asks 'how many' and [zh:点] is the measure word for 'o'clock'. So, [zh:几点] literally means 'how many o'clocks?' and is the standard way to ask 'what time?'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to suggest a time, including 'half past'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Gao Wenzhong suggests a time: [zh:七点半怎么样]. This means 'How about half past seven?'. [zh:七点] is 'seven o'clock', and [zh:半] means 'half'. So, [zh:七点半] is 'seven and a half', or 7:30. He again uses the phrase [zh:怎么样] to make a suggestion and ask for her agreement. This is a very common and useful pattern for making plans.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to confirm a plan and use the phrase 'see you'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Bai Ying'ai agrees and confirms the meeting time. She says, [zh:好,星期四晚上见]. This means 'Okay, see you on Thursday evening.' [zh:好] is 'okay'. [zh:星期四晚上] specifies 'Thursday evening'. The final word, [zh:见], is very important. It means 'to see' or 'to meet'. So the whole phrase is a common way to say 'see you then'.

content.focus_gloss_en: Okay, see you on Thursday evening.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the standard way to say goodbye.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Finally, Gao Wenzhong says goodbye with the classic phrase: [zh:再见]. This is the most common way to say 'goodbye' in Chinese. If we look at the characters, [zh:再] means 'again' and [zh:见] means 'to see'. So, literally, it means 'see you again'. It's a simple and polite way to end a conversation.

content.focus_pinyin: zàijiàn!
content.focus_gloss_en: Goodbye!

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students how to recognize, pronounce, and write the basic Chinese numbers from 0 to 10.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's start with a fundamental building block of Chinese: numbers. Mastering the numbers from zero to ten is essential, as all other numbers are built upon them. We'll look at each character, its pinyin pronunciation, and its English meaning. Pay close attention to the tones, as they are crucial for being understood correctly. For example, the number four, [zh:四], and the number ten, [zh:十], have similar sounds but different tones.

segments.visual_notes: Display a grid of characters. As the narrator speaks, highlight each number card in sequence.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the patterns for forming two-digit numbers in Chinese, up to 100.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now that you know the basic numbers, let's see how to form larger numbers. The system is very logical. For numbers 11 to 19, you use the pattern 'ten plus digit'. For example, fifteen is 'ten-five', [zh:十五]. For multiples of ten, like twenty or thirty, the pattern is 'digit plus ten'. So, forty is 'four-ten', [zh:四十]. For other numbers, you combine these patterns. For instance, eighty-three is 'eight-ten-three', [zh:八十三]. In our dialogue, Gao Wenzhong says he is [zh:十八岁], eighteen years old, which follows the 'ten-eight' pattern.

content.title: Forming Numbers 11–99
content.note: For two hundred, both 二百 (èrbǎi) and 两百 (liǎngbǎi) are used.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Dates - Months & Days of the Week
segments.teaching_goal: To teach the vocabulary for months and days of the week.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's learn how to talk about dates. In Chinese, this is also very systematic. To say the months, you simply combine a number from one to twelve with the word for month, [zh:月]. So, January is 'one-month', [zh:一月], and September is 'nine-month', [zh:九月], as we heard in the dialogue. For days of the week, you use the word [zh:星期] followed by a number from one to six. For example, Monday is [zh:星期一]. The only exception is Sunday, which can be [zh:星期天] or [zh:星期日].

segments.visual_notes: Show two separate grids. First for months, then for days of the week. Highlight 九月 and 星期四 as they are from the dialogue.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the correct word order for stating dates in Chinese and how to ask for the day of the week.

narration_track.subtitle_en: A key rule in Chinese is that time expressions go from the largest unit to the smallest. This is the opposite of English. So, the order is always Year, Month, Day, and then Day of the Week. For example, Friday, July 26, 2019 would be said as '2019 year, 7 month, 26 day, Friday'. To ask 'what day of the week is it?', you use the phrase [zh:星期几]. In the dialogue, Gao Wenzhong asks [zh:九月十二号是星期几], which follows this pattern perfectly: 'September 12th is what day of the week?'

content.focus_text: Q: 九月十二号是星期几？ A: 是星期四。
content.focus_pinyin: jiǔ yuè shí èr hào shì xīng qī jǐ? shì xīng qī sì.
content.focus_gloss_en: Q: What day of the week is September 12th? A: It's Thursday.

grammar_points.pattern: [Year] 年 [Month] 月 [Day] 号/日 星期 [Number]
grammar_points.explanation_en: The standard word order for dates in Chinese, moving from the largest unit (year) to the smallest (day of the week).
The question form to ask for the day of the week. 几 (jǐ) is the question word for numbers.

content.notes: Word Order: Year → Month → Day → Day of the Week

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students how to ask for and state the time in Chinese, using key terms like 点, 半, and 刻.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Telling time in Chinese uses a few key words. The word for 'o'clock' is [zh:点]. For 'half past', you use [zh:半]. So, in the dialogue, when Gao Wenzhong suggests [zh:七点半], he means 'seven-thirty'. To ask 'what time?', you use [zh:几点]. Bai Ying'ai asks [zh:星期四几点], 'Thursday, what time?'. You can also specify parts of the day, like [zh:晚上] for evening, before the time. So, [zh:星期四晚上见] means 'See you Thursday evening'.

content.focus_text: Q: 星期四几点？ A: 七点半怎么样？
content.focus_pinyin: xīngqīsì jǐ diǎn? qī diǎn bàn zěnmeyàng?
content.focus_gloss_en: Q: What time on Thursday? A: How about half past seven?

segments.teaching_goal: To explain when the possessive particle 的 (de) can be omitted.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The particle [zh:的] is used to show possession, similar to 's in English. We see it in the dialogue when Gao Wenzhong says [zh:我的生日], 'my birthday'. However, there's an important exception. When you are talking about a close personal relationship, like family members, you can often omit the [zh:的]. For example, you can say [zh:我妈妈] for 'my mom' instead of [zh:我的妈妈]. But for things like birthdays or professions, the [zh:的] is usually required.

content.focus_pinyin: wǒ de shēngrì
content.focus_gloss_en: my birthday

grammar_points.explanation_en: Standard possessive structure. Example: 我的生日 (wǒ de shēngrì).
The particle 的 (de) is often omitted. Example: 我妈妈 (wǒ māma).

content.notes: Omit 的 (de) for close relationships: 我妈妈 (wǒ māma), but keep it for others: 我的医生 (wǒ de yīshēng).

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the sentence structure for inviting or treating someone to do something.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The verb [zh:请] is very useful. It can mean 'please', but here it means 'to invite' or 'to treat'. The sentence structure is 'Subject + [zh:请] + Object + Verb Phrase'. In the dialogue, Gao Wenzhong says [zh:我星期四请你吃饭]. Here, 'I' is the subject, '[zh:请]' is the main verb, 'you' is the object of [zh:请], and 'eat a meal' is the action he is treating you to. Notice that 'you' is both the one being invited and the one who will perform the action of eating.

content.focus_pinyin: wǒ xīngqīsì qǐng nǐ chīfàn.
content.focus_gloss_en: I'll treat you to a meal on Thursday.

grammar_points.explanation_en: This structure is used when someone treats or invites someone else to do something. The object (O) is both the recipient of the invitation and the subject of the verb phrase (VP).

content.notes: Structure: Subject + 请 (qǐng) + Object + Verb Phrase

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Alternative Questions with 还是 (háishi)
segments.teaching_goal: To teach how to form 'A or B' questions using 还是 (háishi).

narration_track.subtitle_en: To ask a question that offers a choice, you use the word [zh:还是], which means 'or'. The structure is 'Option A + [zh:还是] + Option B?'. In the dialogue, Gao Wenzhong asks [zh:你喜欢吃中国菜还是美国菜], 'Do you like to eat Chinese food or American food?'. This is different from a yes/no question. You are expecting the listener to choose one of the options you provided. It's a very common and useful way to ask questions.

content.focus_gloss_en: Do you like to eat Chinese food or American food?

narration_track.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned in this lesson. We learned how to ask for the date and time, how to state our age, how to invite someone out for a meal, and how to ask questions offering a choice. Take a moment to look at these key phrases. Practice them, and you'll be ready to make plans with your friends in Chinese!
